content.full_text:
Memo to File

August 30, 1976

Re: Dinners Donated by Church Members to UFW Volunteer
Petition Circulators

On August 27, 1976 I informed Nancy Marsh that
pursuant to the facts which she provided the church dinners
did not constitute contributions under the Political Reform
Act. The facts as she relayed them to me are as follows:

These dinners occur on an occasional rather than a
regular basis. They are not in any way intended to be
compensation or a partial substitute for room and board.
These dinners are not used as a platform for recruiting
volunteers nor are volunteers induced by promises of
attending such dinners. These events are social and only
incidental to the political campaign.

The church donates its meeting room and any
kitchen or dining facilities. The food is provided by
church members on a volunteer "potluck basis." The UFW
hoped the Commission would not view these dinners as
political in nature since to do so would discourage
participation by both the church and church members. For a
discussion of possible tax consequences for the church, I
referred her to Rich Eichman as a source of names at the FTB.
With respect to filing obligations by church members, I told
her there would be no filing obligation if the dinners were
not deemed political by the Commission. I told her that
each dinner had to be looked on the basis of its own facts.

I told her that the dinners constituted political
activities if:

1. The event appears to be more political than
social.

2. The event occurs on a regular as opposed to
an occasional basis and, therefore, resembles compensation or
partial room or board.

3. The event is used as an inducement to work
for the organization.


TP:plh

sections.question_synthetic: Whether church-donated dinners to UFW volunteer petition circulators constitute reportable political contributions under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Church dinners do not constitute contributions under the Political Reform Act when they are occasional rather than regular, are purely social in nature, are not used as compensation or inducement for volunteer work, and are donated by church members on a potluck basis without political purpose.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Internal memo format (Memo to File) rather than formal advice letter; no formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections but contains substantive legal guidance; appears to be documentation of oral advice given to Nancy Marsh on August 27, 1976; includes specific criteria for when dinners would be deemed political activities.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether church-donated dinners to UFW volunteer petition circulators constitute reportable political contributions under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: Church dinners do not constitute contributions under the Political Reform Act when they are occasional rather than regular, are purely social in nature, are not used as compensation or inducement for volunteer work, and are donated by church members on a potluck basis without political purpose.

embedding.summary: Memo documenting FPPC advice that church-donated dinners to UFW volunteer petition circulators do not constitute political contributions under the Political Reform Act, provided the dinners remain social and occasional in nature.
